title: Adaptive Preference Measurement with Unstructured Data
abstract: Many products are most meaningfully described using unstructured data such as text or images. Unstructured data are also common in e-commerce, in which products are often described by photos and text but not with standardized sets of attributes. Whereas much is known about how to efficiently measure consumer preferences when products can be meaningfully described by structured attributes, there is scant research on doing the same for unstructured data. This paper introduces a real-time, adaptive survey design framework for measuring preferences over unstructured data, leveraging Bayesian optimization. By adaptively choosing items to display based on uncertainty around a nonparametric utility model, the proposed method maximizes information gain per question, enabling quick estimation of individual-level preferences. The approach operates on embeddings of the unstructured data, thereby eliminating the requirement for manual coding of product attributes. We apply the method to measuring preferences over clothing and highlight its potential for both the general task of marketing research and the specific task of designing customer onboarding surveys to mitigate the cold-start recommendation problem. We also develop methods for interpreting the nonparametric utility functions, which allow us to reconstruct consumer valuations of discrete attributes, even for attributes that were not considered or available a priori.

author.Ryan Dew: The Wharton School, University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania 19104

article.1. Introduction: Consumption decisions often rely on unstructured data such as images, text, and videos. However, despite many tools for measuring preferences over structured attributes (e.g.,Abernethy et al. 2007,Netzer and Srinivasan 2011,Toubia et al. 2012,Huang and Luo 2016), little work has examined how to efficiently measure preferences over unstructured data. This is a critical gap: preference measurement is central to marketing research, and many important product categories, including highly aesthetic products such as clothing and experiential products such as films, are most meaningfully described using unstructured data. The need to use unstructured data to measure preferences extends beyond marketing research. In e-commerce, the need to efficiently measure preferences over unstructured data arises in the context of customer onboarding and the cold-start problem: how can we make good recommendations to new customers? Answering this question involves understanding a new customer’s preferences over a potentially vast assortment of products, which are typically described with text, pictures, and videos but not with a comprehensive set of attributes. The customer onboarding problem is particularly salient for subscription businesses, in which initial recommendations shape whether potential customers decide to sign up, how their preferences evolve subsequently, and whether they decide to churn. To solve this problem, many companies now have onboarding surveys, such as the two shown inFigure 1, wherein new customers are surveyed about their preferences. Crucially, these onboarding surveys must operate in real time and be short enough not to deter customers from signing up. Although such surveys have grown in popularity, there is scant work on how to design them effectively. Thus, in this paper, we propose a new method for adaptive preference measurement that can be applied for designing efficient onboarding surveys and, more generally, for marketing research. Our method combines standard methods for learning embeddings of products with a specially tailored version of Bayesian optimization (BO) (Garnett 2023) to design adaptive surveys for fast preference elicitation. In these surveys, respondents either choose from pairs of items or rate items directly, and the items are adaptively selected to maximize information gain per question based on estimates of respondent-level utility functions, which are modeled using Gaussian processes (GPs). Among the selection rules proposed is a novel heuristic aimed at reducing global uncertainty of this utility function. Crucially, these proposed computations are available in closed form, allowing for the framework to be deployed in real-time as the basis of a survey. The main output is an individual-level estimate of preferences over item embeddings, which can be used for understanding preferences generally (e.g., in the context of marketing research) and for making recommendations based on those preferences (e.g., to solve the onboarding problem). A key benefit of modeling utility in a flexible way over embeddings is the ability to reconstruct consumer valuations of discrete attributes from the learned utility functions even if those attributes were not considered in the original study. We demonstrate the framework using data from a subscription-based clothing retailer. Specifically, we focus on one of its most important product categories, women’s dresses, and use unstructured data in the form of images of those dresses. Through extensive online experimentation, we show that the proposed methodology can meaningfully capture respondents’ preferences, achieving good out-of-sample predictive performance with relatively few adaptively selected questions. We compare the performance of the proposed framework to two benchmarks: a nearest neighbor approach, mirroring the way collaborative filtering algorithms often work, and an adaptive linear model proposed byAbernethy et al. (2007). The results illustrate the importance of adaptivity in accelerating learning and the importance of a nonparametric utility specification for accurate predictions. We also explore the impact that different methods for creating embeddings have on the learning process. Finally, we show how the results can be used for marketing research. Although a nonparametric model of consumer utility improves preference prediction, it also makes interpreting the results challenging. We overcome this challenge by adapting methods from interpretable machine learning and by developing a novel attribute importance metric based on inverse probability weighting. Our work relates to several streams of research. Foremost, it contributes to a long history of research on preference measurement, including conjoint analysis and its many variants (e.g.,Green and Srinivasan 1978,Netzer et al. 2008,Toubia 2018). Whereas nearly all conjoint methods assume products based on describing products with discrete attributes, several recent papers propose ways of integrating unstructured data, includingKim and Allenby (2022), who propose a method for integrating textual data to explain the results of conjoint studies, andSisodia et al. (2024), who develop a way of learning attributes directly from image data but still assume products can be meaningfully expressed through attributes. Perhaps closest to our method isKang et al. (2019), who propose an algorithm for preference measurement based on product geometry and 3D rendering applied in the context of car preferences. Although interesting, their approach relies on being able to 3D render product prototypes and is, thus, less general than the method proposed here. Beyond preference measurement, our work also contributes to a growing literature on incorporating unstructured (and, in particular, visual) data in marketing research more broadly, including recent contributions fromBurnap et al. (2023), who propose a method for understanding and developing new product designs from images, andDzyabura et al. (2023), who show how product image data can be useful for predicting returns. Finally, we contribute a new method to the literature on active learning and optimal experimental design for marketing research, which includes recent work byDzyabura and Hauser (2011),Huang and Luo (2016), andJoo et al. (2019). The rest of the paper is organized as follows. InSection 2, we describe the proposed methodology in more detail. InSection 3, we describe the studies used to test the framework and present the results, focusing first on predictive validity relative to benchmarks and then on how the results can be interpreted, focusing on market research applications. We conclude inSection 4with a summary and discussion.
article.2. Methodology: Our goal is to create a survey based on unstructured product data to understand respondents’ preferences using as few questions as possible. We propose a three-part solution: (1) before collecting any preference data, embed each product in a low-dimensional vector space such that similar products have similar embeddings; (2) model respondent-level utility over those embeddings nonparametrically, using Gaussian processes; (3) gather preference data by asking respondents to either choose from pairs of items or rate items directly, for which the specific items displayed are chosen to maximally reduce uncertainty about the respondent’s utility. Importantly, these components are connected: the nature of the data in (1) determines how we model utility in (2), and the model in (2) guides how data are collected in (3) as uncertainty depends on the model. We describe each of the three steps separately below and include pseudocode for the full method at the end of the section inAlgorithm 1. 2.1. Product Embeddings To begin, we assume the researcher (or company) has access to unstructured product data. To infer respondents’ preferences over these products, we first embed this data in a low-dimensional vector space such that these vector representations meaningfully capture preference-relevant product information. We index products byj=1,…,J, denote the original data byxj, and denote the embeddings (or representations) byzj, wherezj∈RDandDis chosen by the researcher. Although the unstructured data can be anything—text, images, videos, attribute tags, or a mix of these—we focus on the case in whichxjis a product image. Embedding unstructured data in a low-dimensional vector space is standard in many machine learning applications. Our contribution is not in proposing new ways of embedding products, but in highlighting how existing embedding methods can be leveraged in preference measurement. Specifically, we focus on two types of embedding methods, which have different potential uses: indirect methods learn embeddings of products from past purchase data, whereas direct methods learn embeddings directly from product images,xj. As we describe in more detail below, the indirect approach is potentially more powerful as it implicitly encodes preference information. On the other hand, the direct approach is more broad as it only requires product images. Thus, an established firm designing an onboarding survey would likely favor the former, whereas a market research study without prior purchasing data can only rely on the latter.1 2.1.1. Indirect Methods. Using existing purchase data, a simple way to learn vector representations for the products is matrix factorization (MF). MF models purchasing through dot products such that the probability of personipurchasing productjis proportional toui·zj, whereuiis a length-Dvector representing the preferences of personi, andzjis a length-Dvector representing productj(e.g.,Koren et al. 2009,Gopalan et al. 2015). An alternative to MF is neural collaborative filtering (NCF) (He et al. 2017), which generalizes the dot product to a general functionf(ui,zj), trained using a deep neural network.2In both MF and NCF,zjis learned without reference toxj; this is what we mean by indirect. Instead, the embeddings are learned implicitly by looking at which items tend to be purchased by which people. This property makes such representations especially useful in our preference learning framework as they capture preference-relevant product traits. Crucially, the customers whose data are used for learning these representations need not be the same as the customers being surveyed, meaning such representations can be learned using historical data. 2.1.2. Direct Methods. Although past purchase data from other customers is commonly available in the context of e-commerce and customer onboarding, it may not be available in other contexts. Absent such data, we must learnzjdirectly fromxj. There are many methods for doing so, including custom-built deep learning architectures (e.g.,Liu et al. 2020) and deep generative models such as variational autoencoders (Kingma and Welling 2013). In this project, we take a simpler approach: transfer learning using pretrained models. Specifically, we take a deep learning model that was trained for an unrelated purpose and use its internal representations to create embeddings of our images. This approach is advantageous because of its simplicity and its minimal data requirements: because the model is pretrained, we avoid the steep data requirements of training our own representation learning model. Moreover, prior research suggests that the internal activations of large image models can produce fairly general representations of images, which have been used to predict outcomes as diverse as product return rates (Dzyabura et al. 2023) and influencer follower counts (Tian et al. 2024). The pretrained model we use in this project is VGG-19 (Simonyan and Zisserman 2014), which we combine with principal component analysis (PCA) to create low-dimensional product embeddings.3 2.1.3. Extensions to Other Data Types. Although the discussion above focuses on the case of image data, these methods can easily generalize to other data types. The indirect approach, by definition, does not use the product features and, thus, is independent of how the products are described. Direct approaches exist for many types of data: for instance, topic models (Blei et al. 2003), pretrained embeddings (Pennington et al. 2014), and deep generative models (Cheng et al. 2022) can be used for textual data. Deep generative models can also be extended to multimodal contexts, combining text, images, and videos (e.g.,Dew et al. 2022,Tian et al. 2024). 2.2. Modeling Utility over Embeddings Given embeddingszjfor each product, we now propose a way of modeling individual consumer preferences over those embeddings. To develop the model, suppose that we have already run a survey in which personiratedn=1,…,Nproducts with higher ratings corresponding to higher preference. Letjnbe the index of thenth rated item and denote the ratings byyijn. We focus on existing ratings data to simplify the exposition. How to optimally elicit theN+ 1th rating, given this model and history, is the subject of the next section, and how to generalize beyond ratings to binary choices is discussed inSection 2.4. We assume that personi’s ratings are generated (stochastically) based on an underlying utility function over item embeddings, denotedui(z)such thatui(zj)>ui(zj′)suggests the consumer prefers itemjoverj′. There are three key desiderata in modelingui(z): first, unlike in traditional preference measurement studies (i.e., conjoint), in which products are expressed in terms of discrete attributes,zis continuous. Depending on the representation, the dimensions ofzmay interact in determining preferences, and there is no reason to assume preferences will be linear inz. Thus, our model forui(z)must be flexible enough to capture potential interactions and nonlinearities. Second, becauseui(z)forms the basis of a live survey engine, we must be able to updateui(z)on the fly as new data arrive with minimal computational overhead. Finally, as will become more clear in the next section, to useui(z)as the basis of adaptive question selection, we must be able to meaningfully characterize our uncertainty over it and compute that uncertainty in real time. To satisfy these properties, we propose modelingui(z)as a GP. Gaussian processes offer a Bayesian nonparametric approach to specifying uncertainty over unknown functions (Rasmussen and Williams 2006). Denotedui(z)∼GP(mi(z),ki(z,z′)),a GP is defined by two objects: a mean function,mi(z), which captures the expected position of the function, and a covariance function,ki(z,z′), also called the kernel, which captures the relationship between function values given inputs. Conditioned on a set of inputsZi={zij1,…,zijN}, this is equivalent to modeling whereηiis called the amplitude, andρiis called the length scale. The squared exponential kernel is among the simplest and most widely used kernels from the GP literature. Using it implies that the utility function is a smooth function with the degree of smoothness captured byρiand variability of function values captured byηi.5The parametersρiandηiare called the kernel hyperparameters, and we denote them collectively asθi. Returning to our three desiderata, GPs naturally capture flexible functional forms across multidimensional inputs. When using the squared exponential kernel, the inputs can flexibly interact in determining the output; the only assumption is that the function values change smoothly with the distance between inputs. Given their stochastic nature, GPs provide a mechanism capturing uncertainty inui(z). Finally, and most importantly, certain GP models have posteriors that can be derived in closed form. Specifically, suppose we have a series of ratingsyi=(yij1,…,yijN)of items with representationsZi. We assume the ratings are generated with some noise such that Then, the posterior predictive distribution for the utility of a new product with embeddingz,ui(z)is given by Under the previous assumptions,mi(z)=0, andkirefers to the squared exponential kernel with hyperparametersθi.Ki(z,Zi)denotes the length-Nvector formed by evaluatingkipairwise atzand allzj∈Zi.Irefers to the(N×N)identity matrix. Conditional onθiandσi, these analytic expressions allow easy on-the-fly computation of the posterior ofui(z), which can be updated after each rating is given. A remaining issue is how to estimate the hyperparameters,θiandσi. As we can see fromEquation (6), these parameters play a central role in quantifying the uncertainty aroundui(z), which, in turn, plays an important role in adaptive question design as described subsequently. Although a fully Bayesian treatment of these hyperparameters would be ideal for proper uncertainty quantification, even approximate Bayesian methods are too computationally expensive for use on most survey platforms. Hence, we propose two alternative strategies to optimize the hyperparameters: using marginal maximum a posteriori (MAP) inference for online learning of individual-level hyperparameters or learning a set of optimal aggregate hyperparameters from a pretest. Crucially, both of these methods are fast and easy to implement with the former involving simple optimization of a closed-form objective and the latter involving no online computation at all. We give more details about hyperparameter optimization in Online Appendix B. 2.3. Adaptive Queries In the previous section, we specified a way of estimating the utility functionui(z)given ratings of different products. Now, we propose a method for optimally deciding which products to show participants to learnui(z)as efficiently as possible. For notational simplicity, we suppress the subscriptiin this section. The proposed method is based on BO, a method for optimizing black box systems when the objective function is unknown and expensive to evaluate (Kushner 1964,Jones et al. 1998,Garnett 2023). BO has been applied across a wide variety of domains, including for designing molecules in chemistry (Gómez-Bombarelli et al. 2018), improving safety in robotics (Berkenkamp et al. 2023), and optimizing hyperparameters in machine learning models (Snoek et al. 2012). Given a model of the objective function, which in the literature is often a GP, BO defines an acquisition function that determines which points to query, typically with the goal of exploring the objective function and exploiting areas of good performance. Unlike standard BO, in the context considered here, there is no real notion of exploitation as there is no cost to showing respondents items they dislike, and the goal is to effectively learn the function itself, not necessarily to optimize it. Nonetheless, much of the BO machinery can be adapted to our purpose as we describe subsequently. The proposed approach begins by initializing an estimate ofu(z)using a set ofN0randomly selected products.6Once the model is initialized, subsequent questions are designed adaptively. Specifically, we propose two strategies for doing so: maximum uncertainty (MU) and kernelized uncertainty reduction (KUR). 2.3.1. Maximum Uncertainty. The first strategy is what we term MU selection.Equation (6)givess¯i(z|Zi), the uncertainty aboutu(z)for any given value ofz. If our goal is to quickly learnu(z), an intuitive approach is to query the points about which we are most uncertain. That is, givenn− 1 prior ratings, for items with representationsZ:(n−1), thenth item is selected simply by This approach maximally reduces our local uncertainty aboutu(z). In this case,s¯(zj|Z:(n−1))serves a similar role to the acquisition function in BO: it is derived from the posterior estimate of the function and tells us which point to acquire next. However, unlike typical BO applications, in this setting, the possible query points are limited to existing items. Despite this difference, we use the acquisition function terminology going forward and denote the MU acquisition function asaMU(z)≡s¯(z). We illustrate the MU rule visually inFigure 2, in which, in the left panel, we plot a simulated, one-dimensional utility function estimated byEquations (4)–(6) given four observations denoted by asterisks. The gray ribbon around the estimated posterior mean foru(z)is the uncertainty band given by[−2s¯(z),2s¯(z)]. The circles on thex-axis represent possible query points (i.e., previously unrated items). At right, we plot the MU acquisition function, which is justs¯(z). Under MU, the next question would query preferences at point D, which is where the MU acquisition function is highest, corresponding to the query point at which uncertainty is the largest. 2.3.2. Kernelized Uncertainty Reduction. Although MU is easy to use, it is also local: it finds the single item about which we are most uncertain but does not consider how resolving that uncertainty will affect our uncertainty about other items. In fact, our GP assumption foru(z)implies that resolving uncertainty about a pointzwill have an effect on the uncertainty aboutz′as determined by our assumed covariance function. This observation inspires another possible acquisition strategy: selecting the item that will maximally reduce our uncertainty about all remaining items. To that end, we introduce what we term the KUR acquisition function given by Intuitivelys¯(zj′|Z:(n−1))is the amount of uncertainty at pointzj′, whereask(zj,zj′)captures how much an observation atzjwould affect uncertainty atzj′.7Thus, the items with high KUR are those that are themselves uncertain but are also close to other possible test points that are uncertain. The difference between the next item selected by MU versus KUR is highlighted inFigure 2. For very large item assortments, KUR can become costly to compute: not only must it be computed for every remaining (i.e., previously unseen)zj, it also involves either storing the full kernel matrixK(Z∖Z:(n−1),Z∖Z:(n−1))(which requiresO(J2)storage) or a loop to compute the sum inEquation (8). There are two ways we can quickly and straightforwardly approximate KUR: Random selection of test points:Equation (8)is a sum over all points that have not yet been queried, which yields the total uncertainty over the remaining items. We can approximate that by considering a random sample of points to test instead and measure uncertainty reduction over that random set. MU heuristic for potential query points: Another approximation is, rather than evaluating the acquisition function for every previously unqueried point, we can limit ourselves to a set of points that are likely to be good candidates. A simple way of determining potentially good candidates is through the MU acquisition function: that is, we can look at the uncertainty associated with all the points (which is, in any case, required to compute KUR) and limit computingaKUR(zj)tozjthat are sufficiently uncertain (e.g., the top 1,000 most uncertain points). Intuitively, being highly uncertain atzjis a prerequisite for being uncertain aroundzj, and thus, by filtering first on only points that are themselves uncertain, we can reduce the number of times we need to evaluateEquation (8). We refer to this method as the MU heuristic. 2.3.3. Connections to Other Acquisition Functions. The closest analogies in the BO literature to our two proposed acquisition functions are the general class of entropy search acquisition functions: in entropy search, the acquisition function is defined as maximizing information gain, in the information-theoretic sense, for some quantity of interest (Hennig and Schuler 2012). Typically, that quantity is the location of an extremum (e.g., the max). This information-theoretic framing of the problem also arises in the context of Bayesian experimental design (Chaloner and Verdinelli 1995) and, more generally, in the context of active learning (Settles 2010,Jamieson et al. 2015), which has seen some prior use in the marketing and preference measurement literatures (e.g.,Dzyabura and Hauser 2011). Although elegant, entropy search acquisition functions often involve intractable integrals and computationally costly approximations to those integrals. In our setting, because the set of possible query points is finite, directly computing quantities such as uncertainty reduction over the set is possible, bypassing those approximations, which, in turn, makes the methods here easier to deploy at scale on survey engines. Our maximum uncertainty acquisition function is also related to the upper confidence bound (UCB) acquisition function, commonly used in optimization tasks (Srinivas et al. 2010). In UCB, points are selected based on maximizing theαconfidence bound around the estimated objective function. Using our notation, UCB is given by 2.4. Extension to Binary Choices Until now, we have described the setting in which we elicit ratings of items from respondents. Most modern approaches to preference measurement, however, are based on choices. There are important differences between the two: whereas ratings data provide a potentially stronger statistical signal, choice-based questionnaires are often preferred for their closer resemblance to real-world decision making.8In this section, we describe how to adapt the proposed framework to binary choices. A further extension to metric paired comparisons is described in Online Appendix E. Extending the framework to handle binary choices requires making a slightly different assumption aboutui(z): rather than assumingui(z)captures the utility of a single item, we assume utility is now defined over differences between item representations. More specifically, we precompute a new set of pairwisezvalues, denotedzj,j′≡zj−zj′. We then assumeuiis defined overzj,j′such that, ifui(zj,j′)<0, it implies the respondent prefers itemjto itemj′and vice versa forui(zj,j′)>0. To link choices to utility, we code the choices as ratings. Specifically, if the respondent is shown(j,j′)and selectsj′, we code that as a ratingr, whereris an arbitrarily chosen value (e.g., one). Otherwise, if they selectj, the rating is −r. This approach is the same as inAbernethy et al. (2007)and mimics the standard linear probability model. Although explicitly modeling the response as a binary choice through, for example, a logistic link, is possible, the key benefit of our approach is computational efficiency: closed-form expressions for GP posteriors and for the marginal likelihood of GP hyperparameters both rely on assuming the problem takes the form of GP regression with a Gaussian likelihood. Using a different likelihood, such as a Bernoulli for binary choice, breaks these closed-form updates and, thus, would increase the computational burden, making it infeasible for real-time use on computationally constrained platforms. A final consideration is that the ordering of items in a pair is arbitrary, and hence, this new pairwiseui(z)should be symmetric in the sense thatui(z)=−ui(−z). We implicitly encode this information by assuming that, whenever we see a ratingrfor pair(j,j′)with difference vectorzj,j′, we also see the rating −rfor pair(j′,j)with difference vectorzj′,j=−zj,j′. This assumption also simplifies the precomputation of the pairwise difference vectors as we only need to compute one difference vector per unique pair. To simplify exposition going forward, we index unique pairs of products bypsuch thatzpcaptures the pairwise difference vector for pairp. This symmetric observation assumption also affects how we think about KUR: now, for every question, two symmetric points are evaluated. To adjust KUR to this case, we consider uncertainty reduction in two steps: first, from observing a rating atzp, then from observing a rating at−zp. These two steps yield This two-step equation ensures that the approximation does not double count the uncertainty reduction from the first observation. With these modifications, the rest of the framework remains unchanged from the ratings-based case, including how we approximate KUR.9 (Adaptive Preference Measurement, Binary Choice) Data:Precomputed pairwise difference vectorszpfor product pairsp=1,…,P, the number of initial ratingsN0, and the number of total ratingsN Ask respondent to choose fromN0initial pairs,j1,j2,…,jN0, chosen at random Optimize GP hyperparameters (or use preoptimized aggregate values) Compute initial estimate ofu(z)usingEquation (4) forn=1,…,Ndo Select next pair for user to choose from bypn=argmaxpa(zp) Show user pairpn, obtain choice, convert to ratingyn∈{−r,r} (Optional) Use the full history of ratings to optimize GP hyperparameters Update estimate ofu(z)usingEquation (4)and the full history of ratings Update acquisition functiona(z)based onu(z) returnUser’s utility functionu(z).
article.3. Application: Preferences over Dresses: To evaluate our method, we collaborated with Nuuly, a fashion subscription business, which provided us data for one of its most popular product categories: dresses.10Specifically, it provided product data for 815 dresses together with embeddings learned from the recommendation system. As with other subscription box businesses, Nuuly sends boxes of items to subscribers each month and, thus, has a goal of learning, for a new subscriber, what types of products to include in the first box. It also creates the garments it sells and, thus, has an interest in understanding customer preferences with an eye to product design. We illustrate the framework for both building an effective onboarding survey and the more general task of understanding customer preferences over dresses. Using this product data, we tested the performance of the framework on Amazon Mechanical Turk. For each study, we recruited 150 participants. Across studies, several factors were varied, including how the questions were selected (adaptively or not), the question type (binary choice, ratings, or metric paired comparisons), the model used for predicting preferences, and the embeddings. All models were implemented through a Python-based web application. An example of a task is shown inFigure 3. To measure response quality, we included attention checks in the form of randomly redisplaying prior items and only include data for respondents who gave consistent responses across redisplays.11 After finishing a set number of training tasks, each respondent was shown test tasks to assess if the model had effectively learned the respondent’s preferences. The model was not updated during this phase, and the test tasks consisted of previously unseen items. Specifically, the test phase involved 15 evaluations in total of two types: (1) 10 randomly chosen pairs (or items) and (2) five recommendation pairs (or items) predicted to have highu(z). The former assesses the model’s ability to measure preferences generally, whereas the latter measures the model to actively select items based onu(z). For ratings-based tasks, the recommendation task is equivalent to recommending high-utility items, whereas for binary choice, it is equivalent to showing pairs in which the model is most certain about which item will be chosen. 3.1. Results: Binary Choice For the remainder of this section, we focus on the results from the binary choice studies. We include results from ratings and metric paired comparison tasks in Online Appendices D and E, respectively. We tested five models in the binary choice task: Nearest: Random pairs of items are shown to respondents during the training phase. Predictions for new pairs are made as in collaborative filtering, using a nearest neighbor rule: given prior ratingsynfor pairszpn,n=1,…,N, the prediction for a new pairz′isy^=ynfor thensuch that‖z′−zpn‖is smallest. Adaptive linear: This model is based onAbernethy et al. (2007)and is an adaptive linear model, in which the model itself is ridge regression (to allow for cases with fewer ratings than parameters), and items are selected to minimize uncertainty and retain utility balance. MU: This is the GP-based model described inSection 2with individual-level hyperparameters learned by MAP (see Online Appendix B, Equation (A-2)). Items are selected using the MU acquisition function. MU-Agg: This is the same as MU but with aggregate hyperparameters learned from a pretest. Specifically, the pretest was the random item data gathered from the Nearest study. KUR: This is the same as MU but with the KUR acquisition function. Because the number of pairwise combinations is large, KUR was implemented with the two approximations described inSection 2.3. For all models, the representations used for the items were from the company’s recommendation system. Specifically, the company uses a neural collaborative filtering model, in which each item is represented by a 10-dimensional vector. We discuss different representations inSection 3.2. 3.1.1. Predicting Choice for Random Pairs. InTable 1, we show the average respondent-level accuracy for each model for predicting choice in random test pairs. We also show the fraction of respondents for whom the model was able to predict preference above chance. The results suggest that there are sizable gains from the proposed framework: the adaptive GP-based models outperformed both benchmarks and were the only models able to predict preference better than chance. We further highlight the strong individual-level performance of our framework inFigure 4, in which we show the full distribution of respondent-level accuracies across the two benchmarks and the best performing proposed model, MU-Agg. We see that, for the vast majority of respondents, MU-Agg was able to meaningfully learn their preferences: not only are most people above 0.5 accuracy, we see many people for whom the model was able to perfectly (or near perfectly) predict their choices. Comparing among the proposed models, we see MU-Agg performed best. It is worth noting that MU-Agg requires pretest data, whereas MU and KUR do not. That KUR outperforms MU suggests there are gains from considering global uncertainty reduction. Taken together, these results demonstrate the ability of the proposed framework to learn individual-level preferences from simple choices between pictures. Table 1.Average Accuracy, Random Pairs Table 1.Average Accuracy, Random Pairs Notes.Average of the individual-level fit statistics on the test data for each model with standard errors in parentheses. Accuracy refers to the average accuracy in predicting preference in random test pairs. Above 0.5 is the fraction of respondents for whom the model was able to predict preference above 50% accuracy. 3.1.2. Learning Rates. The previous statistics were calculated using the full set of 40 training ratings per user. However, in many contexts, a 40-question survey is onerous. Thus, we now consider how performance of the model changes with the number of training ratings. Specifically, for each respondent, for each study, we retrained the model, using only the firstnratings. Then, we recomputed the accuracies on the test set.12InFigure 5, we plot the results, applying locally estimated scatterplot smoothing (LOESS) to aid interpretability. There are two key takeaways: first, the adaptive nonlinear models exhibit faster learning in early stages than the nonadaptive and linear models. Second, this fast learning tends to level off around 20 ratings, at which point the proposed models achieve close to their peak performance. Although there are some gains past 20, this finding suggests that preferences can be accurately inferred using as few as 20 questions. 3.1.3. Gains from Adaptivity. Although the prior analyses show the potential of the GP-based adaptive framework, it remains unclear to what extent the performance gains are driven by the GP model versus the adaptively chosen items. To answer this question, we conducted another offline analysis, in which we used the responses to the randomly selected items from the Nearest study to fit a GP model with the aggregate hyperparameters, which we then used to predict responses to the random test pairs. This analysis exactly captures what would happen if we turned off adaptivity but kept the GP utility model. We call this offline model GP-Agg Random (for randomly selected items) and relabel the real model GP-Agg Adaptive for contrast. We compare the learning rates of GP-Agg Random to both Nearest and GP-Agg Adaptive inFigure 6. Predictably, the performance of the GP model with random items falls between Nearest and the GP with adaptive items: the gains from Nearest to GP-Agg Random show the benefits of the GP model versus a naive nearest neighbor approach, whereas the gains from GP-Agg Random to GP-Agg Adaptive (i.e., MU-Agg) show the benefits of adaptivity. We see that both matter. The role of adaptivity is to enable faster learning: we see that the adaptive item selection helps the most in the early stages of training. This result implies that, for designing short, effective surveys, adaptivity plays an important role. However, explicitly interpolating between item representations in a nonlinear fashion via the GP also plays an important role in predictive accuracy. 3.1.4. Making Recommendations. As a final validation, we consider how each model is able to actively identify items that respondents will like or dislike. This task is especially relevant for the onboarding problem, in which, to make an initial recommendation to a customer, we only need to be able to identify a few items the customer will surely like. As described previously, during the test phase, respondents were shown five recommendation tasks. These tasks were generated by predicting which of the unseen pairs had the highest values ofu(z)and then showing these to respondents. For binary choice, such pairs represent highly polarizing items:u(z)will be highest when the model is most sure which of the pair will be chosen. To assess performance, we again computed accuracy. The results are shown inTable 2. Echoing previous results, the Nearest model does barely above chance, whereas the highest performing model, MU-Agg, can find polarizing pairs with more than 90% accuracy. The comparison between the two suggests there is a sizable gain from explicitly interpolating utility between items: if the best recommendations come merely from finding the neighbors of previously highly rated pairs, we would expect to see the Nearest model performing better. These strong results for actively identifying polarizing items highlight the potential of the framework for building effective onboarding surveys. Table 2.Average Recommendation Accuracy by Model Table 2.Average Recommendation Accuracy by Model Notes.Average accuracy by model for finding polarizing pairs with standard errors in parentheses. Similar toTable 1, above 0.5 is the fraction of respondents for whom the model was able to make this prediction above chance. 3.2. Different Embeddings Until now, we have focused on results using the company’s recommendation system embeddings. Although such representations are useful insofar as they already capture preference information, they are only available in contexts with prior purchasing data. Thus, in this section, we explore how embeddings directly learned from product images affect the performance of the model. As described inSection 3.2and Online Appendix A, our transfer-learned embeddings are created by combining a pretrained deep learning model (VGG-19) with dimensionality reduction (PCA). To ensure comparability with the original embeddings, we reduce the dimensionality toD= 10. For this section, we refer to the old embeddings as RecSys, and the new as VGG-PCA-10. We also explore additional embeddings in Online Appendix F. We test the performance of the model with the new embeddings in two ways. First, we use an offline analysis, in which we fix the same items and ratings from the MU-Agg study but refit the model using the VGG-PCA-10 embeddings. Second, we run a new (online) study, using the new embeddings in a live application with real respondents. Whereas the online analysis is the more realistic test, the offline analysis helps us understand the role of the embeddings themselves on predictive accuracy, fixing the items shown. We present the results in two ways: first, inFigure 7, we compare the distributions of individual-level accuracies across the online studies. Second, inFigure 8, we compare the learning rates, including the offline analysis. Not surprisingly, we find that the framework performs better with the RecSys embeddings than VGG-PCA-10. The RecSys embeddings already incorporate prior preference information and are, thus, more relevant to predicting preferences. That being said, the VGG-PCA-10 embeddings still yield decent individual-level performance, achieving above-chance accuracy for the majority of respondents. From the learning rates, we see that, as before, the new embeddings tend to level off at around 20 ratings, but the learning rate is overall more modest than the learning rate with RecSys. These findings suggest that even simple transfer-learned embeddings requiring no supplemental purchasing data can yield meaningful results, which greatly expands the applicability of our framework. 3.3. Interpreting Preferences Finally, we consider how we can interpret the learned preferences. Whereas merely learning what items a person likes and dislikes is enough for solving the onboarding problem, for segmenting the market, or designing new products, we need to be able to interpret consumers’ preferences and the extent of heterogeneity therein. In conjoint analysis, such analyses are straightforward, using the part-worths for each attribute level. Under the proposed framework, not only is utility nonlinear, potentially featuring complex interactions across input dimensions, but the representation space itself is not interpretable. Moreover, even if we could map the representations onto interpretable attributes, the fact that the study is based on a fixed set of items means there may not be images showing clear variation only along any single prespecified attribute. To overcome these challenges, we draw on tools from dimensionality reduction, data visualization, and causal inference. 3.3.1. Preference Maps. First, we consider visualizing preferences and segmenting customers according to those preferences, assuming (as before) that we have no additional product attribute information beyond the images themselves. To do so, we first use the GP model to predict the utility of each item for each respondent.13Then, we use UMAP (McInnes et al. 2018) to map the items’ high-dimensional representations into a two-dimensional space such that items with similar representations are close in the two-dimensional space. By overlaying the predicted preferences onto the two-dimensional map, we can visually detect clusters of products that are high or low utility. To demonstrate, inFigure 9, we show the results for a random respondent for whom the model had high accuracy in the random pairs test. From the figure, we can see that, first, there are clear clusters of liked and disliked products: on the left-hand side, for instance, we see a set of low-utility products, roughly corresponding to floral-patterned, form-fitting dresses. On the right-hand side, we see a cluster of high-utility dresses, corresponding to geometric-patterned, loose-fitting dresses. For each customer, the first stage of the above analysis gives us a rating for each of the potential products. To get a sense of market-level preferences, we can cluster these preference vectors and produce segment-level preference maps. Turning again to the MU-Agg data, we applied K-means to the set of predicted utility vectors. Standard clustering metrics suggest a six-cluster solution. The resulting cluster centers, expressed again in two dimensions via UMAP, are displayed inFigure 10. From the figure, we see not only clear clusters emerging, but can find easily interpretable patterns by inspecting the regions of high and low preference. For instance, the high preference regions of cluster 1 correspond to dresses that tend to have no sleeves and mini length, often with lower necklines, whereas those same dresses tend to be low preference for cluster 5, which tends to instead prefer more flowing dresses with longer sleeves and floral patterns. We include a comprehensive description of the segments in Online Appendix G. 3.3.2. Tagged Attributes. Until now, we have assumed the only data available to us are product images, which is the most general assumption. Now, suppose that, after running our analysis, we are given a set of tags describing the items along interpretable dimensions. For instance, our data provider often manually tags products with attributes such as the style or length of a dress to aid in making recommendations and marketing the products. Given such tags, we want to be able to derive something akin to a part-worth from traditional conjoint, quantifying how much that attribute matters in determining utility.14The challenge is that, in our setting, many aspects of a dress may be correlated: it is virtually impossible to find instances in which we have essentially the same dress differing on a single attribute. Thus, if we simply compare the ratings of all dresses that have an attribute to the ratings of those that do not, we cannot be sure that difference in means is attributable to the attribute. To overcome that challenge, we adopt a technique from causal inference: inverse probability weighting (IPW) (Robins et al. 1994). In inferring causal effects from observational data, IPW is used to reweight observations by their likelihood of receiving a specific treatment. This reweighting effectively rebalances the sample to mitigate differences in the distribution of covariates across different treatment conditions. In our setting, the treatments are the different attributes, the outcome is a respondent’s rating, and the goal is to compute an effect of the attribute on the rating that controls for differences in other aspects of the dress beyond the focal attribute as summarized by the item representations.15Mathematically, for a binary attributek, letXjk= 1 denote that productjhas attributek. We first model the likelihood ofXjk= 1 as a function ofzj, yieldingp(Xjk=1|zj).16Although there are many possible ways to model this propensity, we use logistic regression. Then, for each respondent, we compute the IPW estimate of the effect of that attribute on utility by Note that this is an individual-specific estimate of the effect of the attribute. Hence, this approach allows us to understand how preferences for that attribute vary across respondents.17 To illustrate the usefulness of the IPW estimator, inFigure 11, we plot a few examples of the distributions in attribute effects across respondents (again from the MU-Agg study). The attributes are the length of the dress (mini or maxi), whether the dress has a V-neck or collar, whether the dress has a tunic design, and whether the dress has short sleeves. We find variation in both average effects and heterogeneity across attributes. For instance, respondents, on average, tend to prefer mini versus maxi dresses, but both distributions show substantial heterogeneity with effects ranging from −0.15 to 0.2. In contrast, attributes such as a V-neck and short sleeves show relatively minimal heterogeneity with effects ranging from −0.03 to 0.03. We also see bimodal distributions emerge for many attributes, most notably collared necks and tunic styles, which both have large peaks at zero but also substantial positive effects, suggesting potential preference segments. Notably, these effect estimates are very flexible: they can be constructed post hoc for any attribute, even attributes not explicitly considered before running the survey. This flexibility is a key advantage of the proposed framework even for cases in which structured attributes are available. To illustrate, inFigure 12, we show boxplots of the distribution of the IPW effects for 65 attributes. The figure shows that, although many attributes have some effect on utility, most effects are, on average, quite modest (the raw utility scale in these studies was between −0.5 and 0.5). That being said, the modest effects on average mask substantial heterogeneity for several of the attributes, which may be of interest in designing new products. Crucially, standard methods for conjoint analysis could not accommodate so many features in a single study (withHuang and Luo 2016being a notable exception), again highlighting an advantage of the proposed method.
article.4. Discussion: In this paper, we introduce a framework for efficiently measuring preferences with unstructured data inspired by Bayesian optimization. The framework yields respondent-level nonparametric utility functions over embedded product data, which can be used both for market research and to make personalized recommendations in the context of customer onboarding. A key benefit of the approach is its flexibility: although we demonstrate the use of the framework with images, it can be extended to virtually any type of data that can be embedded into a vector space. In our empirical application, we use two types of representations: those based on recommendation systems, which are especially well-suited for designing onboarding surveys, and those based on transfer learning, which can be used even without any prior purchasing data. Although interpretation of the resulting utility functions is challenging, we show how tools from dimensionality reduction and causal inference can be used to understand the learned preferences with a key benefit that the interpretation can be done post hoc for any attribute even attributes not explicitly considered in the survey. There are a number of possible alternatives and extensions to our framework, which may present opportunities for further improving its performance. First, our treatment of product embeddings is quite simple, especially our pretrained direct approach. Further refining the embedding model by, for example, fine-tuning it with product attribute information or preference data from a pretest may yield more meaningful embeddings, which, in turn, may improve the model’s performance. Second, whereas our method models respondents’ utility through Gaussian processes, there are a number of other models that could be used, including tree-based models (e.g.Chipman et al. 2010), Bayesian neural networks (Neal 1996,Wilson 2020), or other deep learning approaches that accurately capture uncertainty (Gawlikowski et al. 2023). These models may prove useful in cases in which the utility function is not smooth, which is an implicit assumption when using GPs. However, they also pose computational challenges for real-time implementation. Finally, in interpreting our results, we use a simple adaptation of IPW to compute attribute effects. Many alternatives to IPW exist in the causal inference literature, including double machine learning methods (Chernozhukov et al. 2018) that add further robustness to the estimation of causal effects. Adapting these methods instead of IPW may further improve the framework’s insights. Other alternative methods for interpreting the results, including methods such as Shapley additive explanations from the interpretable machine learning literature (Lundberg and Lee 2017), may also prove useful. Although the proposed framework has many benefits, there are also several limitations, which may prove fruitful topics for future research. First, the framework is based on a fixed set of items. A potential avenue of future research is how to combine the adaptive approach here with generative AI, similar to the approach proposed inSisodia et al. (2024), such that optimal query points come from a continuous representation space and the unstructured data in the questions are generated on the fly. We also did not explore how to explicitly model specific attributes alongside continuous representations. An important example of such an attribute is price, which is often the key trade-off in conjoint studies. In the context of onboarding surveys, price is not as important, because customers are signing up for a subscription. But, for more general contexts, price is a key variable to consider. As sums of GPs are themselves GPs, a natural way to incorporate price would be by extending the utility tou(z)+v(Price)and modelingv(·)with a GP. This formulation would allow for seamlessly integrating price into the current framework. Further extensions to the method include explicitly modeling choices rather than approximating them through ratings, optimizing for bundles rather than single products, and hierarchical versions of the model that explicitly take into account other respondents in estimating utility for a focal respondent. Although hierarchical approaches are, as of now, too computationally costly to implement on the fly, future work may investigate how to make them feasible. Thanks to the author's industry partner, Nuuly, especially Alan Rosenwinkel and Tom Szumowski, for providing the data. This project would not have been possible without research assistance from a dedicated and talented team of research assistants, especially Yifan Li, Grant Cho, and Nikhil Kona who developed the web application used for data collection and helped run and analyze many of the studies, and the team at Wharton Research Programming, who helped deploy the online testing framework. Finally, thanks to Asim Ansari, Shin Oblander, Olivier Toubia, and Soham Mahadik for helpful comments. 1For readers familiar with recommendation systems, our direct approaches are analogous to content-based recommendation methods, whereas our indirect approaches are analogous to collaborative filtering-style methods. 2Such models are common in industry. We refer interested readers to the cited works for more details about implementation. 3More specifically, we input each image into a pretrained VGG-19 model and then extract the model’s second-to-last fully connected layer as our starting representation. To reduce the dimensionality of the embedding to a more manageable size, we apply PCA, distilling it to 10 dimensions. We include additional details in Online Appendix A. 4Scalability is often a concern in GP applications as the kernel matrix grows with the number of observations. In our case, we only estimate preferences from a relatively small number of questions, which keeps this computation manageable. 5We refer readers toRasmussen and Williams (2006), chapter 4, for a more detailed discussion on kernel selection. In our empirical application, we tested various kernels; the results were largely invariant to this selection across a wide range of stationary kernels (e.g., ARD, Matérn, and their multiplicative and additive variants). 6In the empirical application,N0=1. More strategic (i.e., nonrandom) selection of initial ratings can also be used and may improve learning. 7It is also possible to directly compute how much uncertainty is resolved in the item set after observing a new rating. Although theoretically interesting, directly computing this quantity, which we refer to as global uncertainty reduction (GUR), is too computationally costly to be used in real-time applications. In Online Appendix C, we describe GUR in more detail and compare it to KUR. 8The relative merits of different response types have been discussed extensively in the preference measurement literature. See, for example,Elrod et al. (1992)andVriens et al. (1998). 9These approximations become especially important when using choice-based surveys as the number of possible pairs is far greater than the number of items. 10Examples of two product images can be seen inFigure 3. 11In all of the studies, more than 70% of participants passed this attention check. 12This offline procedure mirrors what would happen online with the exception of respondent fatigue: because the respondents originally saw more than 40 evaluations, there may be more noise in their test ratings than if they had truly ratedn< 40 pairs. 13To infer item utilities from a paired comparison utility function, we compare all products to a hypothetical zero product. That is, the predicted utilities here areu(z), wherezis just the item’s embedding. Thus, a more precise definition of what is being plotted is the preference of the respondent for that item relative to this hypothetical zero. Note that product representations are zero-centered (or roughly zero-centered in the case of RecSys), and hence, the hypothetical zero product is within the span of the data. 14Note that the goal here is post hoc interpretation. If the attributes are few and known a priori, a standard conjoint analysis would likely be preferable to our method for measuring preferences over them. 15Although there is a clear analogy to the standard IPW setting, it is not an exact analogy: for instance, the ratings in this case are the outcome of a trained model, not something observed directly. Moreover, the exact causal ordering of the representation and the attribute are not clear, which is a problem for standard IPW in the sense that covariates in that setting are assumed to be pretreatment. Thus, our IPW estimator should be seen as an imperfect correction to the naive difference in means estimator. 16The IPW estimator requires some assumptions on these probabilities such thatp(Xjk=0|zj)≠0and likewise forp(Xjk=1|zj). This assumption is equivalent to the overlap assumption in causal inference. 17Although IPW is not new, ours is the first paper, to our knowledge, to adapt this idea to explaining a black-box model.
